nodes.label: Formulation Layer D — Deterministic Dynamics and Theorems 1-3
nodes.summary: Layer D specifies the deterministic coupled-ODE dynamics (D1–D6) of the device state given Layer C's symbolic foundation, and proves three theorems that together establish physics-closed signal-processing completeness: (T1) well-posedness, (T2) operator-level realizability, (T3) input-to-output map is a switched time-varying Volterra operator. Stochastic effects are deferred to Layer S.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D1) — Fast activation dynamics
nodes.summary: τ_x · ẋ(t) = −x(t) + N(W(t)·E[u](t) + δW(t)·E[u](t)). Fast activation dynamics: x(t) is the RC-filtered response of the nonlinearity output.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D2) — Perturbation reset dynamics
nodes.summary: τ_δ · δẆ(t) = −δW(t) + Σ_k a_k b_k^⊤ · δ(t − t_k). δW(t) is a leaky integrator of impulsive rank-1 resets arriving at perturbation event times {t_k}.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D3) — Consolidation dynamics
nodes.summary: τ_W · Ẇ(t) = −W(t) + η · S(x(t), y*(t)) · δW(t). Slow weight W drifts under fitness-weighted perturbation accumulation.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D4) — Archive snapshot event
nodes.summary: W̄(t_k⁺) = W(t_k) at snapshot events t_k. Discrete-time jump decoupled from the continuous dynamics; archives current slow weight.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D5) — Decoder readout
nodes.summary: y(t) = D(x(t)). Instantaneous decoder readout of activation state — comparator array applied to x.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: ODE (D6) — Sensor readout
nodes.summary: s(t) = R(W(t)). On-demand sensor readout of the slow weight matrix via sense-amp with row-column select.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 1 — Well-Posedness of (D1)–(D3)
nodes.summary: For bandlimited finite-energy u, Lipschitz N on operating range, continuous target y*, and specified initial conditions, system (D1)–(D3) has a unique absolutely continuous solution (x(·), W(·), δW(·)) on ℝ₊, with W(t) ∈ 𝒲 for all t > 0.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 1 proof — Step 1: piecewise classical between impulses
nodes.summary: Between impulse arrivals [t_{k-1}, t_k), (D2) reduces to τ_δ · δẆ = −δW with closed-form decay δW(t) = δW(t_{k-1}⁺) · exp(−(t − t_{k-1})/τ_δ). (D1) and (D3) are classical ODEs on each inter-arrival interval.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 1 proof — Step 2: Lipschitz on compact state
nodes.summary: f_x(x,W,δW,t) is Lipschitz in (x,W,δW) uniformly in t on compact sub-intervals, since N is Lipschitz and W, δW are bounded. Picard–Lindelöf gives existence and uniqueness on each sub-interval.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 1 proof — Step 3: W stays bounded (feasibility condition)
nodes.summary: ‖(−W + η S δW)/τ_W‖ ≤ (W_max + η·σ·r)/τ_W since S ≤ 1 and ‖δW‖ ≤ σ²√(nm). Combined with −W/τ_W decay, W(t) ∈ 𝒲 for all t > 0 provided W_max > η σ² √(nm) (feasibility condition).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 1 proof — Step 4: impulse concatenation and uniqueness
nodes.summary: At t = t_k, δW has a finite jump δW(t_k⁺) = δW(t_k⁻) + a_k b_k^⊤; (x,W) continues continuously. Concatenating piecewise-classical solutions gives a global absolutely continuous solution on ℝ₊ with δW having countable jump discontinuities. Uniqueness follows from Picard–Lindelöf per sub-interval plus deterministic jump rule.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 1 remark — Lipschitz constants for three canonical N
nodes.summary: f_kink Lipschitz with constant max(α, α+β); f_sat linear Lipschitz in saturation region; f_sub grows unboundedly and requires explicit domain truncation to [V_SS, V_DD] (physically automatic from silicon rails). Theorem 1 is therefore not trivial for f_sub.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 2 — Operator-Level Realizability
nodes.summary: Every operator appearing in (D1)–(D6) admits an explicit mapping to a CMOS-compatible device primitive. No step in the dynamics requires a computation outside the Layer C operator set. Proof is a constructive mapping table.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 2 proof — Constructive device-realization mapping table
nodes.summary: Table mapping each term in (D1)–(D6) to a CMOS primitive and Layer C definition: E[u]→anti-alias RC capacitor, W·v→Kirchhoff crossbar sum, N→transistor-regime stage, RC filters→node capacitor integrators, Σ_k a_k b_k^⊤ δ(t-t_k)→counter-RNG pulse + Gilbert-cell outer product, S→diff-amp+squarer+subthreshold exp, D→comparator array, R→sense-amp, Σ/Π→flash/MRAM program+read.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Key observation — Derivatives are inverse of RC integrator (physics-closed)
nodes.summary: The three derivatives ẋ, δẆ, Ẇ are not separate operations but the inverse view of I_τ (the RC leaky integrator). An RC filter IS a leaky integrator; differentiation is just the read of a voltage decaying through the RC. No step where a computer differentiates — this is what makes the formulation physics-closed.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 3 — Signal-Processing Completeness (Switched Volterra Representation)
nodes.summary: Central theorem. With W(t) held slowly varying (τ_W ≫ τ_x), the input-output map T_W[u](t) := x(t) is (a) a classical Volterra series for analytic N, or (b) a switched Volterra operator partitioned into polytopes {P_q} for piecewise-linear N. Composition u → E → Volterra/switched Volterra → D (or R) is entirely a classical signal-processing chain — no symbolic, discrete, or lookup step intervenes.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 3 proof — Part (a): Volterra series for analytic N
nodes.summary: Taylor-expand N around bias z₀ = W·E[u](0); substitute into (D1); solve iteratively in powers of input amplitude (Rugh 1981). Kernels: h_1(t;τ;W) = h_x(t−τ)·W·h_E(τ), h_2(t;τ_1,τ_2;W) = (1/2)N''(z₀)·h_x(t−max(τ_1,τ_2))·(W h_E)(τ_1) ⊗ (W h_E)(τ_2); higher orders by recursion. Convergence requires ‖u‖ within a radius determined by N's analytic properties.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 3 proof — Part (b): switched Volterra for piecewise-linear N
nodes.summary: f_kink partitions ℝ into 3 linear regions by thresholds {V_T, V_k}. Region tuple R(t) ∈ {0,1,2}^n takes ≤ 3^n values. In each q, N_q(z) = A_q z + b_q and (D1) becomes an LTI system with kernel h_1^q(t;τ;W) = h_x(t−τ)·A_q·(W+δW)·h_E(τ). Global map is a switched linear Volterra operator with finitely many regions and analog threshold-crossing switching events. δW updates shift region boundaries at each perturbation event.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 3 — Explicit Volterra kernel expressions
nodes.summary: Explicit closed-form expressions for Volterra kernels: 1st-order h_1 = h_x * W * h_E; 2nd-order h_2 involves N''(z₀) with outer-product input; in switched case h_1^q per region with gain A_q and bias b_q. Activation RC impulse response h_x(t) = (1/τ_x) e^{−t/τ_x} 1_{t ≥ 0}.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Theorem 3 remark — Formulation v1 Composition Theorem is a special case
nodes.summary: When E is the binarized encoder, N is the single-kink ReLU limit (V_k=∞), and D is sign, the switched Volterra representation collapses to a single region (sign-of-inner-product) and kernels reduce to XNOR+popcount MAC expressions. Theorem 3 is the general case; Formulation v1 is its specialization.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Section 5 claim — Euler discretization reduces (D1)–(D3) to NN + EGGROLL
nodes.summary: Under uniform time discretization Δt and Euler approximation, (D1)–(D3) reduces to standard layered NN forward pass plus EGGROLL update. Taking Δt = τ_x gives x_{t+Δt} ≈ N(W·E(u_t)) (one-layer forward pass); stacking layers gives multi-layer forward. Consolidation discretized at Δt = τ_W/K_batch gives W_{t+Δt} ≈ (1 − 1/K_batch) W_t + (η/K_batch)·S_t·δW_t — the exact EGGROLL rank-1 update. Upgrades K_batch = τ_W/τ_δ ≈ 50 from motif to formal correspondence.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: AMBIGUOUS-resolution Edge 1 — Punch-Through ↔ Depth as Iterative Operator Flow
nodes.summary: Theorem 3 resolves AMBIGUOUS Edge 1: 'Depth as iterative operator flow' is precisely the composition of per-layer switched Volterra operators. Punch-through provides the threshold that induces region switching. Composition-of-operators (discrete depth) = iteration of the switched Volterra flow (continuous time). Upgrade EXTRACTED 1.0.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: AMBIGUOUS-resolution Edge 2 — Kink Effect ↔ State-Region Dictionary
nodes.summary: Theorem 3 resolves AMBIGUOUS Edge 2: 'State-region dictionary' is the partition {P_q} of input space; each region corresponds to a specific region-tuple R(t) ∈ {0,1,2}^n. Kink effect provides the per-unit threshold that defines the partition boundaries. Upgrade EXTRACTED 1.0.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Open point 1 — Bandwidth/convergence radius of the Volterra series
nodes.summary: The k-th kernel h_k has support width ~k·τ_x. For the series to represent bandlimited inputs faithfully, convergence requires ‖u‖_∞ ≤ u_max with radius dependent on N's analytic structure. Computable but tedious; deferred to Layer S where it also governs noise propagation.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Open point 2 — Uniform consolidation (W slowly varying) assumption
nodes.summary: Theorem 3 holds W constant on the τ_x timescale. Justified by τ_W ≫ τ_x but becomes quantitative only with Layer S noise model. Rigorous proof: perturbation analysis W(t) = W_0 + δ(t) with ‖δ‖ = O(τ_x/τ_W).
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Open point 3 — Switching-event dynamics at region transitions
nodes.summary: Theorem 3(b) glosses over exact dynamics at region transitions. Measure-zero events for generic inputs but may matter under worst-case adversarial input. Deferred.
nodes.author: ws

nodes.label: Open point 4 — Multi-layer region count (Zaslavsky / Hanin-Rolnick)
nodes.summary: A multi-layer device is a cascade of switched Volterra operators, each with its own region partition. Global partition cardinality grows as 3^{Σ n_ℓ}. Connects to State-Region Dictionary's region-count theorem — a Zaslavsky-type hyperplane arrangement bound. Left open until Stage 2 incorporates Hanin-Rolnick.
nodes.author: ws
